metadata.purpose: Fictional cipher with two-step encryption: alphabet shift + 4-character block reversal. Decryption requires applying the inverse operations in reverse order.

nodes.text: The Karth cipher is a fictional two-stage cipher used for short messages. Encryption applies a fixed forward letter shift then reverses every consecutive 4-letter block. Decryption MUST reverse both operations in opposite order: first un-reverse the blocks, then shift letters backward. Skipping the block step turns Karth into a plain Caesar cipher and produces wrong plaintext for any message of length >= 4.
Karth step 1 (forward shift): each plaintext letter is shifted +7 positions in the alphabet, modulo 26. Case is preserved. Non-letters pass through unchanged. Example mapping: A->H, M->T, Z->G.

Karth step 2 (block reversal): the shifted text is split into consecutive 4-character blocks from the left. Each FULL block of 4 is reversed in place. A trailing block of fewer than 4 characters is left in original order. Example: 'OLSSV' splits to 'OLSS' + 'V'; the first block reverses to 'SSLO' and the final result is 'SSLOV'.

Decrypt step 1 (un-reverse blocks): apply the SAME block-reversal procedure to the ciphertext. Reversing twice restores the original order within each full 4-character block, because reversal is its own inverse. The trailing partial block is again untouched.
Decrypt step 2 (inverse shift): each letter is shifted -7 positions in the alphabet (equivalently +19), modulo 26. Case preserved. Example mapping: H->A, T->M, G->Z.

The order is fixed: when decrypting, you MUST un-reverse the blocks FIRST and then apply the inverse shift. Doing the shift first (then trying to un-reverse) does not commute and yields the wrong plaintext for any message of length >= 4.

Worked example (3-letter message, no block reversal applies). Encrypt 'DOG':
Step 1 (+7 shift): D->K, O->V, G->N. Intermediate = 'KVN'.
Step 2 (block reversal): only 3 chars, no full block, so 'KVN' unchanged.
Ciphertext = 'KVN'.

Decrypt 'KVN':
Step 1 (un-reverse blocks): no full block, so 'KVN' unchanged.
Step 2 (-7 shift): K->D, V->O, N->G.
Plaintext = 'DOG'.

Worked example (5-letter message, exercises block reversal). Encrypt 'HELLO':
Step 1 (+7 shift): H->O, E->L, L->S, L->S, O->V. Intermediate = 'OLSSV'.
Step 2 (block reversal): split into 'OLSS' + 'V'. Reverse the full block: 'OLSS' -> 'SSLO'. Trailing 'V' unchanged.
Ciphertext = 'SSLOV'.

Decrypt 'SSLOV':
Step 1 (un-reverse blocks): split 'SSLO' + 'V'. Reverse 'SSLO' -> 'OLSS'. Trailing 'V' unchanged. Intermediate = 'OLSSV'.
Step 2 (-7 shift): O->H, L->E, S->L, S->L, V->O.
Plaintext = 'HELLO'.

MISCONCEPTION: 'Karth is just a Caesar cipher with shift 7; ignore the block step.' This is wrong for any message of length >= 4. Applying only the inverse shift to 'SSLOV' gives 'LLSEO', not 'HELLO'. The block-reversal step is structural; skipping it destroys the plaintext.

Karth cipher: encrypt = (shift +7) then (reverse every 4-char block). Decrypt = (un-reverse every 4-char block) then (shift -7). Order matters; reversal is self-inverse; trailing partial blocks are untouched.
